You know this is one of the most important parts of engagement with Facebook ads is you've got literally a few seconds to get them to take note of your ad and then a few seconds if that to get them to engage with your content as well so whether you're running a video or you've got copy and static and carousel whatever your ad format looks like that engagement piece the first few seconds is so so important so give you an example I ran a tv ad campaign in 2016 2017 and we took the same video from that tv ad and we ran it on Facebook as well and what we actually did was to take the end frames the end slates of the video and actually bring them to the front so we took the payload at the end of the tv ad brought it to the front because essentially on with a tv

## [15:19 - 18:23]

solution to a problem and actually then start to describe this brand new product as well and you know some of the key things to be looking out for in communicating and engaging in your ad is what what are the goals of your user what are they trying to get that you can offer what are their struggles if it's a mum and you've got a product that helps mum to stay organized then the struggle is around organization so how can you focus on organization or maybe it's a false belief maybe there's a product that you're selling or a service that you're selling which is going to help solve a problem for them but they have a different mindset around it so how can you persuade them and bridge the gap between what they believe to be the case which is not the case and you're going to convince them otherwise or perhaps what's the opportunity cost what's the cost of inaction if they don't take your product or your service or your offer what's going to happen you know if you don't buy that lorry ticket the opportunity cost is you're not going to win the lorry so it's kind of thinking about how you can translate that into your products and service and get that point across that if you don't take this action today or if you don't buy this product or if you don't click here you're not going to get the benefits that you want out of things that they're going to lie for the products etc and also kind of things like impulsiveness as well if you've got a low ticket value product then impulsiveness is a big part of convincing people to buy as well so a lot of people who are selling low ticket e-commerce products they they really push for on impulsiveness so you know 20 dollars 30 dollar products it it doesn't need too much thinking to part with that much money and so they play a lot on impulsiveness so using a lot of social proof some really cool videos and trying to get that person to engage and click through as well and one of the most important things I look at when I'm looking at the customer the product and the proposition is maslow's hierarchy so if you haven't heard of this before maslow was a behavioral scientist I guess and he was kind of studying as a psychologist sorry he was studying the studying human behavior and the different qualities that people have and they look for as well and he's created this kind of layered pyramid which goes through the most basic needs so right at the bottom of the pyramid up to things which are aspirational and a lot of it goes also from the physical to the non-physical as well and actually if you relate your product or service to one part of this pyramid it also relates to how you need to communicate the product or service as well so if you're selling something related to food or water or sleep then people know naturally what

## [18:23 - 21:25]

that is you don't need to explain that this is food this is how you consume food this is the benefit of food we kind of all know that or the benefit of sleep people generally know that you have to sleep you've got no choice but it also helps you to regenerate recharge etc so there may be a few things you can skip that as you go up that hierarchy things like self-esteem or confidence or morality or some kind of sense of achievement some maybe you know friendship family all that kind of stuff is a bit more intangible now so when you're describing the product or service you have to try and make it a bit more tangible and relate it to the person you're speaking to so therefore the approach changes and generally what I find is as you go up the hierarchy here you you end up elongating the content the funnel the communication because it takes that much longer or take it's that much harder to actually get your point across as well so if you're selling let's say something which is going to help people become a lot more self-confident self-confident within themselves then you need to take them through a completely different journey to selling food parcels or food packages or whatever it be and and so the kind of copy has to be a bit more inspirational it has to connect a lot better you're probably going to end up using a long copy you're probably going to end up maybe using email capture and taking through an email journey etc so I like to reflect products on the kind of hierarchy of needs as well so it's just something to think about when you're coming up with your ads and part of the brain and kind of a deeper response you're more likely to get someone hooked and engage and then speak to the functional state so here's a dating app or give us your email address we'll John Caple David Ogilvy is a key one as well and what I kind of try and visualize is how you take someone through the journey of the kind of headline and then into the sub headline etc is very similar don't have words for it but in this example here what I've done is to take that kind of flow and that structure and broken it out into a typical kind of news piece now obviously this isn't something necessarily for your ad but give you an example of how you break it out so at the top of your pyramid of your copy you use a few few as as few words as possible so SpaceX sends Tesla into space and it's just about grabbing your attention and then you expand and you add a few more words Helen Musk's SpaceX program delivers his own Tesla into space so you're giving a little bit more information a then take them through the journey you could get away with just one headline if it's a video ad and maybe it's a recognized brand or you're doing kind of warm targeting someone's already seen your ads won't see a problem but there are so many areas where you can can fall foul as well and Facebook over the last year or so have started to really put their foot down on policy so I absolutely recommend ## [24:31 - 26:15]

policy there's a few different levels of rejection number one is a kind of mild rejection so in this example here you might have said you know meet other black singles near you which is against policy and the feedback will say you know ads must not contain content that asserts or implies personal attributes etc etc so that's a kind of mild policy block if for example you are pushing illegal products then that's going to be a harder block and actually Facebook plays places some waiting on this as well so I've been speaking to my rep around the different levels and the general feeling there is the bigger the policy violation the more chance your ad your account has of being blocked and so for example affiliates they often fall foul of this big time affiliates are generally running 10 20 even 50 different accounts at the same time to avoid being shut down as a typical Facebook advertiser or an agency you're running one maybe two accounts one for backup and one is your main but you don't want to fall foul of this you don't lose all your ad set history or your setup and etc etc so do keep an eye on policy and make sure that when you are trying to kind of create this amazingly good content to stop the scroll to create the engagement you're not falling foul of policy as well so this section we've gone through nut number two which is engage and in the next section we're going to talk about convert
